autonomy.name: Autonomy
autonomy.description: How much you delegate to AI and what verification strategy replaces human review at each level.

levels.name: Autocomplete
levels.desc: AI suggests completions, you accept or reject inline. You write the code, AI accelerates typing.
levels.verification: Human verifies inline as they type.
obstacles: Feels like 'just autocomplete' — hard to see it as the first step of a progression
Tab-accepting without reading creates subtle bugs early
No mental model of what the AI is doing behind the scenes
howToProgress: Start noticing when you accept suggestions without reading them
Try giving the AI a whole function to write via chat — observe the difference
Compare your output speed on a task with and without completions

levels.name: Review Every Edit
levels.desc: AI generates code blocks or diffs. You review and approve every single change before it lands.
levels.verification: Human is the full verification layer. Every diff gets eyes.
obstacles: Review fatigue — rubber-stamping after the 20th diff
Output looks plausible, so your brain registers 'good' before you've checked logic
Trusting code that compiles but has subtle logic errors
howToProgress: Set up YOLO mode, so agent can do it's thing, but be safe enough for your use case
Review 100 diffs manually — note where errors actually hide
Experiment by trying to fix the bugs with AI
Start categorizing: which types of prompts are always safe vs. need scrutiny

levels.name: Review Per Session
levels.desc: You let the AI work on a task without human approval, then review the full session output holistically. You use some sort of YOLO mode for that.
levels.verification: Human reviews holistically. Linter, formatter, and basic tests support the review.
obstacles: Missing subtle errors buried in a larger changeset
Losing track of what the AI changed vs. what was already there
Session output too large to review effectively
howToProgress: Use git diff as your review surface instead of watching edits live
Set up linting and formatting to catch mechanical issues automatically
Practice identifying the 'critical path' in a changeset — what matters most
Start writing tests before delegating so you can verify the result

levels.name: Review Critical Only
levels.desc: You only review architecture decisions, security-sensitive code, and critical business logic. Everything else is covered by automated verification.
levels.verification: Automated code review, agentic verification loops, CI/test suites cover non-critical code. Human focuses on what matters.
obstacles: Defining what's 'critical' vs. 'non-critical' is harder than it sounds
Automated review tooling isn't set up yet — you skip review instead of automating it
False confidence in test coverage that doesn't actually catch agent-introduced bugs
Agentic verification loops need their own validation
howToProgress: Set up automated code review (agentic reviewers, linters, static analysis)
Define explicit criteria: what always needs human eyes
Build agentic verification loops — AI reviews AI output
Measure: what percentage of bugs are caught by automation vs. human review
Write tests before delegating tasks so the AI has success criteria

levels.name: Review the Result
levels.desc: You don't look at code. You review the outcome — does the feature work, does the test pass, does the deploy succeed.
levels.verification: Full CI/test pipeline, automated quality gates, staging environment validation. Code is a black box.
obstacles: Technical debt accumulates invisibly when nobody reads the code
Edge cases that pass automated tests but fail in production
Losing architectural coherence across many AI-generated changes
Hard to debug when something eventually does break
howToProgress: Build comprehensive test suites that you trust more than code review
Set up staging environments that mirror production
Implement automated architecture checks (dependency rules, module boundaries)
Schedule periodic deep-dive code audits even if you don't review daily

levels.name: Fully Automated
levels.desc: No human review. Tasks flow in, code ships out. End-to-end automated verification, monitoring, and rollback handle quality.
levels.verification: End-to-end automated verification pipeline, production monitoring, automatic rollback on anomalies. Human intervention is the exception, not the rule.
obstacles: Requires extremely mature testing, monitoring, and rollback infrastructure
Novel failure modes that no automated check anticipates
Organizational trust — stakeholders uncomfortable with no human in the loop
Regulatory requirements may mandate human review in some domains
howToProgress: This is the frontier. Build confidence incrementally by automating review for low-risk tasks first.
Invest in production monitoring and anomaly detection
Design circuit breakers that halt automation when metrics deviate
Maintain the ability to drop back to level 5 or 4 instantly when needed

parallelExecution.name: Parallelism
parallelExecution.description: How many concurrent AI tasks you manage and how they're coordinated.

levels.name: Single Task
levels.desc: One agent, one task, one branch. You wait for it to finish before starting the next thing.
obstacles: Waiting feels natural — hard to see idle time as waste
No concept of task independence — everything feels sequential
The agent finishes fast enough that parallelism seems unnecessary
howToProgress: While waiting for one agent, ask yourself: is there a second independent task I could start?
Time your idle wait across 10 sessions — that's your parallelism opportunity
Identify tasks in your backlog that don't touch the same files

levels.name: Manual Parallel
levels.desc: You run 2-5 agents in parallel on separate tasks. You launch them manually and track their progress yourself.
obstacles: Context switching between agents is cognitively expensive
Parallel tasks that step on each other (same files, merge conflicts)
Losing track of which agent is doing what
howToProgress: Identify 3 truly independent tasks and run them simultaneously for a week
Build a lightweight tracking system for parallel agent state
Learn to decompose work into non-overlapping units
Practice the attention discipline: check in on agents, don't hover

levels.name: Task Queue
levels.desc: You have an AI task orchestrator. You write tasks, the orchestrator picks them up and assigns them to agents automatically.
obstacles: Task specification must be precise enough for autonomous execution
Queue management overhead — stale tasks, priority inversion
Orchestrator errors compound across the queue
howToProgress: Set up a simple task queue (even a file-based one) that agents consume
Write task specs precise enough that any agent can pick them up without clarification
Build monitoring for queue health: stuck tasks, failure rates, throughput

levels.name: Directed Orchestration
levels.desc: You give high-level direction. AI proposes a task breakdown, you review and approve it, then the orchestrator executes.
obstacles: AI task decomposition may miss dependencies or create wrong abstractions
Reviewing proposed tasks requires understanding the full scope
Approval bottleneck — you become the queue's limiting factor
howToProgress: Start with AI proposing task breakdowns for features you understand well — calibrate trust
Build templates for common task decomposition patterns
Define approval criteria: what makes a proposed breakdown good enough to execute
Gradually expand to less familiar areas as you build confidence in the decomposition quality

levels.name: Autonomous Orchestration
levels.desc: You give direction, AI creates tasks and executes them. You define goals and constraints, the system handles decomposition and execution.
obstacles: Wrong decomposition propagates errors across all spawned tasks
Cost explosion without governance — autonomous agents burn tokens freely
Emergent behavior when many agents interact without human checkpoint
Hard to debug: which decision in the chain caused the bad outcome
howToProgress: Build robust monitoring and cost controls before enabling full autonomy
Implement circuit breakers that pause orchestration on anomalies
Start with narrow domains where autonomous decomposition is well-understood
Begin delegating the orchestration policy itself — let the system choose how many agents to spawn

levels.name: Self-Organizing Swarm
levels.desc: The system decides the shape of the swarm. Agents spawn, coordinate, and retire other agents as goals demand — you set outcomes, not task graphs.
obstacles: Runaway scaling — swarms expand faster than budget or rate limits allow
Coordination failures emerge from agent interactions no one scripted
Goals drift subtly as the swarm reinterprets them across generations
Post-hoc explainability: reconstructing why the swarm took a given path
howToProgress: This is the frontier of parallel execution.
Invest in outcome-level observability — measure results, not agent activity
Enforce hard budget and rate caps the swarm cannot override
Keep a kill switch that collapses the swarm to a single-agent fallback
Treat goal specifications as code: version them, test them, review them

skillUsage.name: Skill Usage
skillUsage.description: How sophisticated your AI tooling is — from using what's built in to creating self-improving tool chains.

levels.name: Built-in Only
levels.desc: You use the AI tool's default capabilities. No custom configuration, no extensions, no plugins.
obstacles: Don't know what extensions exist or why you'd want them
Built-in capabilities feel sufficient for current tasks
Configuration feels like yak-shaving
howToProgress: Browse the extension/plugin marketplace for your AI tool
Ask other developers what tools they've added to their setup
Identify one repetitive task that a plugin might automate

levels.name: Third-Party Skills
levels.desc: You install and use third-party plugins, extensions, or skill packs that extend your AI's capabilities.
obstacles: Evaluating quality — many plugins are poorly maintained
Configuration overhead for each new tool
Security concerns with third-party extensions accessing your code
howToProgress: Install 3 well-regarded extensions and use them for a week
Evaluate: which actually saved time vs. which were overhead
Start reading how existing skills are built — peek at the source

levels.name: MCP Services
levels.desc: You connect AI to external services via MCP (Model Context Protocol) — databases, APIs, monitoring tools. The AI can query and act on real systems.
obstacles: MCP setup and configuration can be fiddly
Security implications of giving AI access to external systems
Knowing which services benefit most from MCP integration
howToProgress: Connect one MCP service to your AI tool (e.g., database, monitoring)
Use it for a real task and evaluate the workflow improvement
Think about which of your daily workflows involve systems the AI can't currently reach

levels.name: Create Skills Manually
levels.desc: You write custom skills, commands, or workflows that extend your AI's capabilities for your specific needs.
obstacles: Learning the skill/extension authoring format
Scoping: writing skills that are too broad or too narrow
Maintenance burden of custom tooling
howToProgress: Identify your most repeated AI workflow and codify it as a custom skill
Write 3 skills and iterate based on daily use
Share a skill with a teammate — their feedback reveals your assumptions

levels.name: Create Skills with AI
levels.desc: You use AI to generate and iterate on custom skills. The AI helps build its own tooling.
obstacles: AI-generated skills may have subtle bugs in edge cases
Prompt engineering for skill generation is its own skill
Testing AI-generated tools requires understanding what they should do
howToProgress: Have AI generate a skill from a natural language description of what you need
Iterate: use the skill, find gaps, have AI fix them
Build a feedback loop: use → evaluate → improve → repeat

levels.name: Self-Improving Loop
levels.desc: Skills improve themselves. AI evaluates skill effectiveness, identifies weaknesses, and generates improved versions — a recursive self-improvement cycle.
obstacles: Improvement loops can drift from the original intent
Evaluating whether an 'improvement' is actually better requires clear metrics
Runaway optimization for the wrong objective
Diminishing returns after initial improvements
howToProgress: This is the frontier of skill development.
Define clear success criteria before starting improvement loops
Build in human checkpoints to prevent drift
Track metrics across iterations to know when to stop

safety.name: Stakes
safety.description: The consequence level of your environment. This is not a progression — it's where you operate. It determines how much verification infrastructure you need at each autonomy level.

sandbox.label: Sandbox
sandbox.desc: Hobby projects, personal tools, static sites. Mistakes are annoying but recoverable.

normal.label: Normal
normal.desc: Production systems with real users. Mistakes stop the service, but the blast radius stays bounded.

hardcore.label: Hardcore
hardcore.desc: Business-critical systems. Failures cascade to downstream services and customers.

impossible.label: Impossible
impossible.desc: Life-safety domains — medical devices, autonomous vehicles, critical infrastructure. The highest stakes.
